methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in AI & Software (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 38 same-sector peers exist in AI & Software, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

data.narrative:
Preferred Networks is a Series D+ stage AI & Software company that has raised ¥16.88B to date. The valuation universe for frontier-tech software remains constrained: while 38 peers operate within the AI & Software sector, the comp set for strict stage and valuation-tier matching yielded fewer than three datapoints, rendering traditional peer-median analysis inapplicable. The broader reference set includes one public comparable (Palantir, ticker PLTR) and eight private peers, among them OpenAI, xAI, Anthropic, and Databricks.

The peer-median method was not executable due to insufficient narrow-stage comparables. The fragmented maturity profiles across the reference universe—spanning pre-revenue generative models to established enterprise software operators—creates significant methodological friction. Any single-method reliance here would obscure rather than illuminate valuation drivers. Without triangulated results from multiple applicable approaches, the analysis cannot responsibly propose a specific valuation range or synthesize findings.

The core uncertainty is structural: frontier-tech AI companies at this stage occupy a valuation regime where traditional metrics (revenue multiples, earnings, FCF) often prove uninformative, while comparable transactions and disclosed private rounds remain sparse. A credible valuation would require either (1) expansion of the comp set to include earlier-stage or later-stage AI operators with disclosed valuations, or (2) adoption of scenario-based methods tied to explicit assumptions about TAM, adoption curves, and margin trajectories. Neither is available in the present dataset.
